# Abstract

*Longevity is a defining feature of the Swiss life insurance market, yet rising life expectancy has been accompanied by an increase in the years people spend in poor health. This report quantifies the actuarial impact of these mortality and health trends on insurance product pricing and reserving over the period 1988 to 2024. Using period life tables from the Human Mortality Database (HMD), the study computes the Actuarial Present Value of Benefits (APVB) for Whole Life Insurance, a Deferred Annuity, and Term Mortgage Insurance under a range of technical assumptions. The results point consistently in one direction: mortality improved steadily across the period, the gender gap narrowed without fully closing, deaths became more concentrated at advanced ages, and the survival curve grew increasingly rectangular. The year 2020 stands out as a temporary disruption consistent with the COVID-19 period, after which the 2024 table recovers. The central actuarial implication is that longevity does not affect products uniformly. Under positive interest rates it lowers the present value of death-contingent benefits while raising the expected duration of annuity and pension payments. That divergence explains why Swiss insurers have moved toward hybrid product designs intended to hedge mortality risk against morbidity risk, and why doing so has become a condition of long-term sustainability in an ageing and increasingly dependent population.*

# Task 1

**I. Introduction**

Mortality assumptions — the age-specific probabilities of death that actuaries use to model and project how a population dies — have long formed the financial core of life insurance underwriting and pension mathematics. What has changed is the underlying conception. The field no longer treats survival as a fixed schedule but as a quantity that drifts continuously in one direction, reflecting the secular decline in death rates observed across advanced economies (Pitacco, 2019). Expressed as mortality tables, these assumptions govern both pricing and the mathematical reserves an insurer must hold against long-term obligations. Because future mortality cannot be known with certainty, insurers and pension funds are exposed to longevity risk: the risk that people live longer than was provisioned for. Longer lives are broadly positive, but they create a genuine financial problem for any institution that has guaranteed lifetime income. In annuity portfolios the exposure is systematic and one-sided, since each further improvement in survival lengthens the payout period, inflates the liability, and threatens solvency if the assumptions are left unrevised.

Switzerland offers a particularly clear case for examining these issues together. Over the last century it became a benchmark for public health and economic stability, and that transformation reshaped its demographic structure: death was steadily postponed, and the upper boundary of survival moved outward. The Swiss Federal Statistical Office (FSO) documents this shift in detail. Within the country's multi-pillar retirement system — the state first pillar (AHV/AVS) and the mandatory occupational second pillar (BVG/LPP) — longer lifespans translate fairly directly into financial pressure.

Local actuarial practice has reflected this concern for some time. Work published by Aon Switzerland, together with Swiss Life's technical documentation on the BVG generation tables, indicates that static mortality assumptions fail to capture the velocity of improvement, and that failing to capture it leads to under-reserving. To remain within the capital requirements of the Swiss Solvency Test (SST), providers must continually re-examine how the survival curve is moving rather than treating it as fixed.

The report proceeds in stages. It first quantifies the historical longevity trend over 1988–2024 and sets it against healthy life expectancy, establishing the demographic baseline on which everything else rests. That baseline then feeds the valuation of two core products — Whole Life and a Deferred Annuity — chosen to show how oppositely they react to the same mortality improvement. The analysis then turns to Term Mortgage Insurance, comparing a prudential reserve basis against a realistic one. Taken together, the three components trace the relationship between Switzerland's evolving mortality and the financial durability of its long-term insurance liabilities.

**II. Demographic Baseline and Mortality Trends Analysis**

**2.1 Actuarial Methodology & Data Specification**

The empirical work uses Swiss period life tables in the $1 \times 1$ format (single-year age groups by single-year calendar periods) drawn from the Human Mortality Database (HMD). To capture how longevity changed over time without an unmanageable volume of data, a working dataset of ten periods spaced four years apart from 1988 to 2024 was constructed. The central quantity is the curtate expectation of life ($e_x$) — the average number of complete future years lived by someone who has reached age $x$ — computed in RStudio from the survival function ($l_x$):

$$e_x = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{l_{x+k}}{l_x}$$

Here $l_x$ is the standard survival function, the number of individuals still alive at age $x$ out of a starting cohort of $l_0 = 100{,}000$ births, and ${}_{k}p_x$ is the probability that someone alive at age $x$ survives at least $k$ further years. Two indicators carry most of the analysis: life expectancy at birth ($e_0$) and life expectancy at the conventional retirement age ($e_{65}$). Both are computed and compared across three populations — male, female, and total — for a total of 30 tables.

Beyond the two indicators the assignment requires, the analysis adds the Lexis Point to the trend charts. The rationale is practical: a single mean conceals the shape of old-age mortality, whereas the Lexis Point makes it visible. It rounds out the picture along three dimensions of academic and practical value.

**2.2 Empirical Results ($e_0$ and $e_{65}$)**

The full set of $e_0$ and $e_{65}$ values across all 30 tables has been compiled separately. Given the size of the table, the complete figures are provided in the accompanying spreadsheet **Life_Expectancy_30Tables_Complete_CHE.csv** for verification rather than reproduced here.

**2.3 Longevity Indicators and Methodology**

Three markers, plotted over time, carry this section: life expectancy at birth ($e_0$), total expected lifetime at age 65 ($e_{65} + 65$), and the Lexis Point. Each answers a distinct question.

Life expectancy at birth is the familiar headline figure, but as an arithmetic mean it is unusually sensitive to deaths early in life. Historically, large gains in $e_0$ owed less to pushing back the natural limit of life than to no longer losing infants and young adults. Total expected lifetime at age 65 avoids that distortion: by adding 65 to the conditional expectancy $e_{65}$, it considers only those who have already cleared the premature-death years, isolating old-age survival. The Lexis Point — the adult-old age at which the most deaths occur in the life table — serves as an empirical proxy for the natural biological lifespan.

The third marker is where the analytical value lies. Because $e_0$ is a mean depressed by early deaths, it reveals little about when the typical older person dies. The Lexis Point identifies that age directly: computed from 60 or 65 onward, it is the age at which the elderly death count peaks — in effect, the age most people reach before dying of biological ageing alone, assuming they survived the accidents and early illnesses of youth. Plotting it on the same axes as the two expectancy lines proves to be the clearest way to observe two phenomena at once: the compression of mortality and the rectangularization of the survival curve. It also indicates how closely the average Swiss lifespan has approached the human biological ceiling, which is worth establishing before turning to the year-by-year data.

**2.4 Evolution of Survival Distribution and Mortality Dynamics**

**2.4.1 Female analysis**

![][image1]

Figure:

The survival function ($l_x / l_0$) is the natural starting point for tracing Switzerland's longevity path from 1988 to 2024, since it displays mortality compression at a glance.

Two features stand out, the first being rectangularization. In a population with substantial mortality variability, the survival curve slopes down steadily from childhood. The Swiss curve does not. It holds an almost flat plateau near the maximum survival probability of 1.00 through youth and adulthood and out to roughly age 75, and only then drops sharply and nearly vertically. Comparing 1988 (red line) with 2024 (pink line), the plateau has flattened further and the drop has steepened. The reading is straightforward: idiosyncratic risk has been squeezed out, premature and accidental death in early and middle life has largely been eliminated, and almost the entire cohort now survives into old age, with death packed into a narrow and predictable window.

The second feature is expansion. Alongside the rectangularization, the entire curve has shifted to the right over time, so the area beneath it grows year on year. More people survive to much older ages, and the median lifespan — the age at which survival probability reaches 0.50 — has advanced noticeably deeper over the last decade.

Taken together, the two phenomena capture the central tension of the report: Swiss citizens increasingly die at nearly the same advanced age, clustered around the Lexis Point, yet that age keeps moving outward, and the uncertainty in how fast it moves is precisely the aggregate longevity risk insurers carry. The same concentration of survivors at older ages is the mechanical reason dependency ratios are rising, and it underscores why the recent pension reforms became necessary.

To examine the lifespan distribution directly, the analysis turns to the curve of deaths ($d_x = l_x - l_{x+1}$), the empirical distribution of individual lifetimes. The Swiss plots — total, segmented by cohort, and zoomed in on early life — give a multi-angle view of the same compression from 1988 to 2024.

![][image2]

Figure:

The aggregate female death curve is the classic skewed bell shape, and it moves considerably over the period. There is a spike at age 0 for infant mortality, then a drop to near zero through youth and early adulthood, then an exponential climb through older ages to a tall peak — the Lexis Point — before it falls away as the biological limit is reached. Tracking 1988 (red line) to 2024 (pink line) shows two things happening together: the peak shifts rightward, advancing toward age 90, and it grows taller and narrower. That narrowing is the definition of mortality compression — the spread of ages at death is shrinking, and lifespans are being funnelled into a tight window at the oldest edge of life.

The segmented and zoomed plots reveal the engine behind the compression.

![][image3]

Figure:

The clearest single piece of evidence is the collapse of the infant-mortality spike at age 0 across the decades. In 1988 (red line) it sits at its highest; by 2024 (pink line) it has flattened almost to the baseline. Switzerland's historical gains in $e_0$ were driven heavily by eliminating early-life deaths through strong neonatal and paediatric care. The Swiss National Health Report 2020 (Obsan) attributes this directly to long-term investment in paediatric medicine and neonatal networks across the cantons.

![][image4]

Figure:

Once early-life deaths are minimised, more people reach old age and the population concentrates in the 60-plus segment. The right panel of the facet plot shows the senior death cluster around the Lexis Point intensifying rapidly. This is the empirical form of the framework's argument: modern socio-medical progress has removed the idiosyncratic risks of early and middle life, rectangularizing the curve because people end up dying at nearly the same age, while the upward climb of that peak is what continues to raise the aggregate longevity risk bearing on the national pension system.

The final component is the age-specific mortality probability ($q_x = d_x / l_x$), which tracks how the annual risk of dying shifts at each exact age from 1988 to 2024. It traces the familiar J-shape (or U-shape) observed across human populations.

![][image5]

Figure

The Swiss $q_x$ profiles maintain a consistent biological structure across the years. There is a small bump at age 0, after which the curve falls to its lifetime minimum and stays flat and near zero through youth and middle age — a sign of how successfully premature and external hazards have been avoided. Past age 60 the curve bends upward and climbs exponentially, reflecting biological senescence as the body loses resilience.

The notable change over time appears in the 60-to-90 band, where the curve has moved down and to the right. In effect, a 75-year-old faced a lower annual chance of dying in 2024 than in 1988. That steady reduction in old-age $q_x$ is the mechanism that rectangularizes the survival curve, holding the population intact until the biological wall, and it is the same mechanism that stretches the expected total lifetime at 65 ($e_{65} + 65$) outward.

One detail matters for pricing: at the extreme ages (95+) the curves more or less overlap. Medicine has deferred death among the younger-old but has not moved the ultimate ceiling. That is the macroeconomic bind in a single picture — suppressing $q_x$ at senior ages compresses mortality into the oldest years, yet the uncertainty in how far and how fast that suppression proceeds is the longevity risk that complicates the pricing of Swiss annuities.

To prepare for the projection work, the 2024 Swiss mortality is fitted on a log scale, comparing the classic Gompertz model (orange line) with the Gompertz–Makeham refinement (teal line) against the empirical points (black dots).

The Gompertz model assumes mortality rises exponentially with age from senescence alone, which appears as a straight line on a log scale. Across ages 50 to 90 the Swiss points sit almost exactly on that line, confirming that elderly mortality is essentially all biological.

![][image6]

Figure:

Gompertz–Makeham adds a constant term for age-independent background mortality — accidents, environmental hazards, acute infection — which appears as a flat floor at younger ages rather than the straight line continuing downward. Notably, the 2024 Swiss points fall below that Makeham floor and align with the plain Gompertz line instead. The regression confirms this: the Makeham parameter ($c$) drops toward statistical insignificance in 2024, meaning background hazards now barely register. This aligns with the preventive-health framing in Martinović et al. (2024), in which systems like Switzerland's suppress environmental risk to the point that mortality is dominated almost entirely by senescence — the same rectangularization and compression result arriving from a different direction.

**2.4.2 Male analysis**

![][image7]

Figure:

The female curve reached its flat, rectangular shape relatively early. The male curve in 1988 (red line) did not — it shows a long, continuous downward drain through early and middle adulthood (ages 30 to 60), the signature of heavy premature mortality. By 2024 (pink line) that middle-age slope has essentially disappeared. The male plateau has lifted to meet the female standard, holding flat near 1.00 before the same sharp, cliff-edge fall after age 75. The point is that male rectangularization arrived later but progressed faster, compressing a comparable transformation into a shorter span of years.

![][image8]

Figure

![][image9]

Figure:

The redistribution of male deaths ($d_x$) provides the most direct evidence of compression. The magnified dx_young_0_20 panel shows both sexes eliminated the age-0 infant spike, but the adult distributions diverge sharply. In 1988 the adult male death curve was wide, low, and skewed left — lifespans were highly dispersed. By 2024 that wide spread had collapsed into a much taller, much narrower peak at age 90.

![][image10]

Figure:

A gap of roughly 3.5 years remains between male and female $e_0$, but the $d_x$ curves reveal what the means conceal: the ultimate biological ceiling is synchronising. In 1988 the modal ages at death sat far apart (Lexis 83 for men, 86 for women). By 2024 both curves have compressed onto the 90–91 boundary — 90 for men, 91 for women. Regardless of sex, lifespans in modern Switzerland now concentrate in nearly the same window at the end of life, against what appears to be a shared biological wall.

The male convergence was driven by the suppression of male $q_x$. Over the period the J-curve for men was pulled down sharply across the 15-to-50 band, erasing the historical "accident hump" — the bulge of young-male deaths from accidents and external causes that was always prominent in male profiles and largely absent in female ones.

![][image11]

Figure:

The Gompertz–Makeham fit for 2024 confirms this. Past age 50 the male points track the straight orange Gompertz line almost exactly, so senior male mortality is now pure senescence. More striking, at younger adult ages the points sit safely below the Makeham floor. The implication is significant: Switzerland has driven background environmental risk down so far that the male population has shifted into an almost purely senescent mortality structure, which is what closed the historical gap between the sexes.

![][image12]

Figure:

The same driver, stated plainly: suppressing male $q_x$ across the 15-to-50 band removed the accident hump that was once a male-only feature. As the model shows, senior male points (50+) now sit on the linear Gompertz slope while young-adult risk falls entirely below the Makeham baseline.

![][image13]

Figure:

Over the decades the 1988 accident hump was flattened out. By 2024 the male $q_x$ curve runs smoothly, with no artificial spike, and the young-adult risk profile has become a clean baseline mirroring the female one. The eradication reflects the effect of Swiss public safety, workplace regulation, and mental-health provision: young male survival has been decoupled from environmental and behavioural hazard. Rather than being lost early, the modern male cohort moves safely into old age, where mortality is again almost entirely senescent.

**2.5 Quantitative trends in Curtate Life Expectancy**

**2.5.1 Female analysis**

![][image14]

Figure:

The Swiss female figures from 1988 to 2024 offer a clear demonstration of advanced longevity: steady growth, stable structure, and clear convergence.

The headline indicator comes first. Female $e_0$ climbs continuously and almost linearly — 80.23 years in 1988, 84.57 by 2020, peaking at 85.40 in 2024. A gain of more than 5 years at birth indicates that public health, medicine, and living conditions continued to compound benefits and remove premature risk across the whole life course.

The conditional marker $e_{65} + 65$ moves in step: 83.97 years in 1988, 86.68 in 2020, 87.46 in 2024. Its continued rise matters, because it shows the longevity gains are not merely a historical artefact of saving infants. People who have already survived the hazards of youth and adulthood are still gaining years in old age.

The Lexis Point tells the biological story. Across the period it stays locked at a very advanced age, drifting tightly between 86 and 91 — 86 years in 1988, up to 91 by 2008, holding at 91 in 2024. Sitting that high indicates that natural ageing is the dominant cause of female death, with most deaths clustered around 90, and that premature causes — accidents, epidemics, poor conditions — have been almost entirely removed by the quality of Swiss life and healthcare.

The principal insight from the chart is the convergence of the three markers. The gap between $e_0$ and the upper indicators ($e_{65}+65$ and the Lexis Point) has narrowed substantially. In 1988 the distance between $e_0$ (80.23) and the Lexis Point (86.00) was 5.77 years; by 2024, with $e_0$ at 85.40 and the Lexis Point at 91.00, the absolute gap closed slightly while the two expectancy lines moved very close together.

That tight, synchronised upward movement is strong evidence of mortality compression and rectangularization among Swiss women. Individual lifespans have become highly predictable — accidental and premature death has been systematically removed — and nearly the whole generation now survives in good order up to the natural biological boundary.

**2.5.2 Male analysis**

While the female trajectory is one of stable, high-baseline longevity, the male trajectory is a more pronounced structural catch-up.

![][image15]

Figure:

In 1988 men carried a heavy survival deficit: $e_0$ of 73.40 years, nearly 7 years behind women (80.23). Male $e_0$ then climbed rapidly — 80.50 by 2020, 81.92 by 2024. That cumulative gain of 8.52 years runs about 1.6 times the female pace (5.17 years) and steadily closed the historical gender gap. The same pattern appears in $e_{65} + 65$, up from 79.80 to 84.94 years. The male Lexis Point advanced sharply as well, from 83.00 (1988) to 90.00 (2024), now just one year behind the female anchor of 91.00.

The asymmetry is the salient point. Women extended their lifespans through passive extension; men underwent an active, intensive optimisation. The gap between male $e_0$ and the male Lexis Point shrank from 9.60 years (1988) to 8.08 years (2024). That rapid narrowing reflects how Swiss socioeconomic and workplace-safety frameworks disproportionately protected a historically vulnerable male workforce, engineering out the middle-age risks that once spread male deaths so widely.

**2.5.3 Both analysis**

![][image16]

Bringing both sexes together — with the national Lexis Point locked at the 90–91 band and the overall survival curve ($l_x$) now firmly rectangular — yields a broader socioeconomic conclusion. Lifespan extension in Switzerland is no longer a gendered phenomenon but a universal, institutionalised structural reality.

Classic pension-fund modelling relies on mortality variance to remain solvent. The combined data shows Switzerland has instead entered a state of extreme lifespan uniformity. With premature death engineered out and the whole population compressed into a narrow window around age 90, that variance has collapsed. The synchronisation removes the mortality "liquidity" pension funds historically relied on, which directly explains the financial pressure on the AHV old-age system, and forms the empirical backdrop to Switzerland's recent pension reforms — essentially an attempt to keep the fund solvent against a population of near-centenarians.

**2.6 Recent context and policy updates for Switzerland**

The rectangularization and compression demonstrated above are not abstractions; they are the force behind Switzerland's current legislative and fiscal challenges. When a whole cohort survives uniformly into advanced old age and draws benefits well past 65, a pay-as-you-go system runs structural deficits simply because the payout period is so long.

To stabilise the first-pillar fund — the AHV (Alters- und Hinterlassenenversicherung) — Switzerland has enacted reforms whose key phases fall in 2025 and 2026, combining the ongoing AHV 21 reform with new financing measures.

The core of AHV 21 targets precisely the gender-specific longevity in the data. It harmonises the reference retirement age by raising women's from 64 to 65. According to the Federal Social Insurance Office (FSIO), the change proceeds in three-month annual steps: 64 years and 3 months in 2025, 64 years and 6 months in 2026, reaching full parity with men at 65 by 2028. The effect is to keep more people in the labour force, offsetting the longer span of pension dependency.

In addition, the voter-approved 13th monthly AHV pension — due for full implementation in December 2026 — acts as a structural shock requiring secondary financing. The Federal Council's timeline confirms the extra month will be legally mandated and disbursed for the first time in December 2026. Federal financial reports warn that it will place immediate cash-flow pressure on the social-insurance segment, prompting a VAT increase to 8.4% to secure the fund. The through-line is that suppressing $q_x$ and rectangularizing the life tables are not isolated demographic curiosities but determinants of national fiscal planning, turning life-table analysis into a tool for macroeconomic survival.

# Task 2

**III. Healthy life expectancy vs total life expectancy**

To assess Switzerland's demographic position up to 2024 properly, total life expectancy is insufficient on its own. This section sets it against health-adjusted figures from the World Health Organization (WHO, 2024), so that the years gained can be divided into healthy years and years lived with morbidity.

On the WHO baseline, the long-run gain in raw longevity is substantial. Total Life Expectancy (TLE) at birth for the whole Swiss population rose by 3.61 years in two decades, from 79.7 years in 2000 to 83.3 years in 2021. Disaggregated by sex, a Swiss girl born in 2021 can expect 85.1 years and a boy 81.5.

| ![][image17] | ![][image18] |
| :---- | :---- |

For context, that places Switzerland 7.0 years above the European regional average of 76.3 years — a wide margin.

The problem emerges when the lens shifts to Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE/HALE) at birth, which counts only the years lived in full health, without disabling morbidity or loss of autonomy. While TLE rose 3.61 years, HLE rose only 2.80 (from 68.4 years in 2000 to 71.1 in 2021). Subtracting one from the other reveals a deficit: the average Swiss citizen now spends 12.2 years living with chronic old-age morbidity and functional limitation, up from 11.3 years in 2000.

|  | ![][image19] |
| :---- | :---- |

That period of post-healthy dependency is longer than the European average of 10.3 years. In modelling terms, the widening gap between TLE and HLE is exactly what the "pandemic theory" of senescent disability predicts (Pitacco, 2014). Rather than compressing morbidity, Switzerland is caught in the paradox that better healthcare lowers mortality ($q_x$) and extends life without lowering the rate of chronic old-age disability. The WHO cause-of-death data bears this out.

Non-communicable, degenerative, age-dependent conditions dominate the Swiss health profile. Ischaemic heart disease is the leading cause of death (104.5 per 100,000 for women, 116.7 for men), followed closely by Alzheimer's and related dementias (99.1 per 100,000 for women, 48.2 for men).

The chronic, drawn-out nature of these conditions — neurodegenerative disease, progressive cardiovascular illness — is precisely what compresses the healthy years. These are not conditions from which people die quickly; thanks to advanced Swiss care, patients survive with them for years past 65 in states of chronic morbidity and cognitive decline.

For an insurer or a national scheme carrying aggregate longevity risk, this represents an undiversified, systemic threat. The exposure is not only paying annuities for longer but the simultaneous drain from health products and long-term care demand as people spend more time in the disability state.

This is also the structural justification for the legislative turn taken on 1 January 2024. It is the demographic reason the Confederation brought AHV 21 into force and began raising the women's reference age from 64 to 65: women retain relatively stable, disability-free function around ages 64–65, just before the steep onset of chronic disease, so the policy uses that healthy labour window to stabilise the first pillar.

The same long post-retirement morbidity period — roughly 14 years on the gendered figures — is also why the 13th monthly pension, first paid in December 2026, arrives as such a heavy cash-flow shock. Actuarial projections cannot treat longevity as a single number; they must model a double burden, in which ageing citizens draw pensions and consume heavy healthcare resources simultaneously in their final years. Comparing TLE against HLE up to 2024 makes the same point as Task 1 from the health side: life-table analysis is the foundation for the country's ongoing fiscal restructuring and pension re-engineering.

# Task 3

**IV. Life insurance market**

**4.1 Market Size of the Swiss Life Insurance Sector**

The Swiss private insurance sector is a major pillar of the country's finances, operating within a strict, formalised framework supervised by the Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (FINMA, 2025). Defining its size requires being clear about institutional boundaries within the three-pillar system. This analysis covers only the private commercial carriers under FINMA — which captures the insurance-linked parts of occupational pensions (second pillar, BVG/LPP) and voluntary private provision (third pillar). It deliberately excludes the state-run, pay-as-you-go first pillar (AHV/IV), which never appears on a commercial balance sheet.

The market itself is mature and consolidated: roughly 18 licensed life insurers write direct business (ASA-SVV). To measure scale, the analysis uses Gross Written Premiums (GWP) as the annual flow and technical liabilities as the accumulated stock of obligations (Dickson et al., 2020).

On FINMA's latest disclosures, total premium across the whole Swiss insurance market reached CHF 150.0 billion (FINMA, 2025). Within that, life insurance alone accounted for CHF 23.666 billion in GWP. That figure declined 8.4% on the year, but the decline was technical — foreign branches being converted into independent foreign subsidiaries — and underlying profitability remained strong, with life-insurer annual profit up 21.7% to CHF 1.587 billion (FINMA, 2025).

Annual GWP captures new business, but the truer measure of size is the stock of obligations on the balance sheet, since that ties directly to long-duration longevity risk. Swiss life insurers hold an enormous base: a balance-sheet total of CHF 290.236 billion, total investments of CHF 267.558 billion, and gross technical liabilities of CHF 252.269 billion (FINMA, 2025).

The composition shows how tightly the commercial market is wired into the mandatory second pillar. Group occupational pension business accounts for CHF 14.375 billion, or 60.7% of total life GWP. Gross actuarial reserves stand at CHF 216.372 billion, of which group pension schemes make up 57.8% (FINMA, 2025).

That capital is managed by a dense, top-heavy field. The six largest life insurers control 85.5% of direct Swiss life premium. Swiss Life leads decisively with 41.0%, followed by Helvetia Leben (12.4%), Baloise Leben (10.2%), AXA Leben (9.0%), Allianz Suisse Leben (7.2%) and Zurich Leben (5.7%) (FINMA, 2025). The concentration indicates that the market runs on well-capitalised institutions able to carry a large reserve base under the SST capital buffers — the life sector's average SST ratio held at a strong 227% (FINMA, 2025).

**4.2 Penetration rate**

The insurance penetration rate is the standard macro indicator of how deep an insurance sector runs relative to the economy: annual GWP divided by GDP (Dickson, Hardy, & Waters, 2020).

$$\text{Penetration Rate} = \left( \frac{\text{Gross Written Premiums (GWP)}}{\text{GDP}} \right) \times 100\%$$

The ratio measures how much risk has been transferred from households and firms onto insurers. In Switzerland it is more than a statistic — it is a gauge of how far the social security system leans on commercial carriers.

On FINMA data (2025), total penetration (life plus non-life) sits stably between 9.0% and 10.1% of GDP. Life alone runs around 3.1% to 3.4% (Insurance Annual Report, 2024), corresponding to the CHF 23.666 billion of GWP. Setting aside the technical fluctuation from the foreign-branch restructuring, these figures confirm a "hyper-mature" market in which life insurers act as institutional investors with real weight in the national capital market.

The longer history shows a structural decline. FINMA and Swiss Insurance Association (SIA) data put the rate at 5.5%–6.0% in the early 2000s, well above today's level. Two factors drove it down. First, the prolonged stretch of low interest rates made traditional savings-type life products far less attractive. Second, an institutional shift occurred within the second pillar: companies moved from "full insurance" toward "semi-autonomous" pension foundations, taking on investment risk through their own funds instead of buying complete insurance packages. As that capital decoupled from insurers' books, the numerator in the penetration ratio shrank.

The fall in penetration is not a fall in importance, however — it reflects a change in business model in response to longevity risk. The pivot toward unit-linked products and wealth management is a robust adaptation to demographics. Gross technical liabilities of CHF 252.269 billion alongside an industry-average SST ratio of 227% (FINMA, 2025) are evidence that even with GWP lagging GDP growth, Swiss insurers maintain exceptionally strong capital buffers — a stable footing for the actuarial and pension-sustainability modelling that follows.

**4.3 Long-term care products**

Long-term care (LTC) in Switzerland does not resemble the large standalone product market found in the United States. It is a specialised segment integrated into the broader social security system. Under the Federal Health Insurance Act (KVG/LAMal), the public system funds basic nursing needs, so commercial LTC products are built as supplementary cover (*Zusatzversicherungen*) — bridging the convenience costs above statutory limits or covering home-care services outside the mandatory scope (Federal Office of Public Health, 2025). In practice this makes private LTC a wealth-protection instrument that allows high-net-worth individuals to avoid depleting personal assets before they qualify for state-subsidised support.

Structurally, Swiss LTC tends to be embedded in life contracts via accelerated death benefit (ADB) riders. Under SIA actuarial standards (2024), the trigger is not subjective — it must follow national clinical assessment systems such as BESA or the Resident Assessment Instrument (RAI), which keeps payouts transparent. The products generally use level premiums to manage long-term risk, which obliges insurers to hold substantial technical reserves against both longevity and morbidity. FINMA oversees this closely, classifying LTC obligations as high-duration, high-volatility liabilities under the SST (FINMA, 2025).

The most difficult problem for Swiss actuaries here is care-cost inflation combined with the correlation between longevity and frailty. FINMA's market reports (2025) note that as life expectancy rises, time spent in the frailty state lengthens as well, which strains the funds. Unlike ordinary consumer inflation, wage costs in Swiss nursing grow faster than the bond returns insurers earn on their portfolios, which is why the sector increasingly needs frailty tables rather than mortality tables alone. The shift from family-based care to professional services is raising demand for private LTC, pushing insurers to restructure portfolios continually so the industry-average SST ratio remains safe, currently around 227% (FINMA, 2025).

# Task 4

**V. Longevity Trends and Pricing Evolution**

This part values two basic life contracts for the Swiss population — Whole Life Insurance and a Deferred Life Annuity (ordinary) — using historical life tables from the Human Mortality Database (HMD).

The aim is to compute the Actuarial Present Value (APV) of each set of benefits across several decades (1988–2024) and then determine how structural mortality improvement moves the insurer's obligations in opposite directions for the two products. The method follows standard actuarial practice: fix the financial and demographic assumptions, then discount the life-contingent cash flows. The result is a concrete illustration of why dynamic mortality modelling matters for pricing rather than being an optional refinement.

**5.1 Whole life insurance**

**5.1.1 Methodology and Assumptions**

The valuation is deterministic and targets the APVB — the fair price of the promised benefits. For statistical weight it uses 10 life tables spanning 1988 to 2024 at 4-year intervals. The model assumes a technical interest rate of 2.50% per year ($v = 1 / (1 + 0.025)$) for discounting and a policyholder entering at age 35. It is run separately for males, females, and the total population to keep the demographic differences visible. The benefit is assumed paid at the end of the year of death, so:

$$APVB = Benefit \times \sum_{k=0}^{\omega-x-1} v^{k+1} \cdot {}_kp_x \cdot q_{x+k}$$

The sum assured is fixed at 400,000 EUR. With that fixed, any movement in APVB derives from mortality, which is precisely what the analysis aims to isolate.

**5.1.2 Results and Data Representation**

The APVB results for the Swiss population across the 10 mortality points are compiled in the spreadsheet **"APVB_WholeLife_Switzerland_Results"**, the primary data source here. The headline is a consistent downward drift in APVB, which is what mortality improvement and the resulting deferral of expected payouts should produce over three decades. At a technical rate of 2.50%, the inverse link between rising life expectancy and the present value of a death-contingent obligation comes through clearly. The visual trends are discussed below.

**5.1.3 Actuarial Analysis and Discussion**

**![][image20]**

Figure:

From 1988 to 2024 the APVB falls across every population series — the fair value of Whole Life cover has been steadily declining. The curves run roughly parallel, indicating that the mortality improvement is broad-based rather than confined to one group.

The driver is structural mortality improvement in Switzerland. The APVB for a death benefit discounts future payments by the probability of surviving ($p_x$) and dying ($q_x$), and the real-world backdrop supports the trend: Switzerland operates one of the most advanced health systems anywhere, with broad access and one of the highest health-spending shares of GDP globally. Sustained investment in cardiovascular screening, chronic-disease management, and early diagnostics has cut preventable deaths substantially.

Financially this is the time value of money at work. With discounting fixed at 2.5%, the further out a payment moves, the less it is worth today. Longevity therefore acts as a built-in discount: it lowers the reserve the insurer needs to hold. People live longer, the benefit is expected to be paid later, and the present obligation shrinks.

One clear deviation appears around 2020 — an upward movement in the trend line that reads as an exogenous shock, almost certainly COVID-19. Swiss mortality rose temporarily, and because a Whole Life benefit pays on death, a mortality spike raises the chance of earlier claims, weakens the discounting effect, and lifts the APVB for that year.

It is a deviation, not a reversal. By 2024 the series returns to the downward path, confirming that the long-run longevity gains remain the dominant force on cost. For Swiss insurers, this sensitivity is the case for dynamic mortality modelling: static pricing misses these shifts and can leave a book exposed to adverse selection, undermining the pool.

The chart also shows a stable ordering — males require the highest APVB, females the lowest, and the total population in between. This is not a statistical accident; it reflects the demographic and biological facts already visible in the $q_x$, $l_x$, and $d_x$ charts in Task 1. Male $q_x$ runs consistently above female at any age, for reasons that combine biology and behaviour. Biologically, oestrogen's cardiovascular protection lowers female heart-disease incidence in mid-life. Behaviourally — and the FSO documents this — Swiss men smoke and drink more and work in physically riskier occupations, while Swiss women tend to be more proactive about screening and chronic-condition management, where men more often delay care until symptoms are severe. The net effect is a higher probability of earlier male death, which feeds directly into the actuarial cost of the 400,000 EUR benefit.

In actuarial terms, higher male mortality means the death benefit is expected to be paid sooner, less interest accrues against it, and the present value of the obligation is correspondingly higher. Higher female survival defers the expected payout, allows discounting to work longer, and yields a lower APVB. The more important dynamic, however, is the gradual convergence over 1988–2024. The male APVB remains on top, but its downward slope is markedly steeper than the female one, so the gender gap continues to narrow.

FSO data supports this: Swiss mortality improvements have come from better healthcare, healthier lifestyles, and stronger prevention, with developments in cardiovascular treatment, early diagnosis, and public-health programmes cutting premature death especially among men. As the gaps in life expectancy and lifestyle close, the corresponding actuarial risks align, which is why the APVB curves draw together and trend toward uniformity by 2024. The total-population curve, being a weighted average, naturally sits in the middle.

The relationship between mortality improvement and pricing comes down to how sensitive the cash flows are to survival probability, and for Whole Life it is clearly inverse — as mortality improves, APVB falls.

The formula makes the mechanism explicit. When mortality improves, survival ${}_kp_x$ at younger and middle ages rises, pushing the expected timing of death — and the payout — further out. Since the present value uses $v^{k+1}$, a longer deferral means a lower value today. The time value of money works in the insurer's favour, because the claim lands in later years where the discount bites harder.

That improvement effectively provides insurers with an implicit safety margin. Pricing Whole Life on dynamic tables captures the compounding effect of longevity: every additional year a policyholder lives is a year of premium collected without a claim, with interest accruing on the reserves. This strengthens solvency and cushions volatility — a real advantage in a market with Switzerland's regulatory capital demands.

For the Swiss market, then, the APVB trend is a strategic signal rather than a statistical curiosity. The downward path means static pricing on outdated tables is a source of model risk: insurers who fail to bank the mortality gains end up overcharging, which invites adverse selection as lower-risk policyholders leave and the pool deteriorates. Dynamic adjustment is what keeps premiums competitive while keeping the pool sustainable.

In summary, for Whole Life Insurance, mortality improvement is a positive financial driver that lowers the present value of liabilities — but only if the insurer measures the timing of death accurately. The lesson for Switzerland is that pricing must move from static historical benchmarks to dynamic, predictive modelling to reflect the structural shift toward longer lives.

**5.2 Deferred Life Annuity Ordinary**

**5.2.1 Methodology and Assumptions**

The annuity is the mirror image. Where Whole Life uses mortality improvement as a buffer by deferring payment, a deferred annuity is directly exposed to longevity risk. The contract here pays an annual benefit (B) of EUR 50,000, is issued at age 25 (x), has a 40-year deferral period (m), and uses a technical rate of 1.75%. The APVB at $t=0$ combines the pure endowment factor with the present value of the annuity:

$$APVB_{ann} = B \cdot {}_{m}E_{x} \cdot a_{x+m}$$

The pure endowment factor ${}_{m}E_x = v^m \cdot {}_{m}p_x$ combines the discounting with the probability of surviving the deferral period, while $a_{x+m}$ is the present value of the life annuity payments that begin once deferral ends.

Under the equivalence principle, the level periodic premium $P$ equates the expected present value of premiums to the APVB:

$$P = \frac{APVB_{ann}}{\ddot{a}_{x:\overline{m}|}}$$

where

$$\ddot{a}_{x:\overline{m}|} = \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} v^k \cdot {}_kp_x$$

**5.2.2 Results and Data Representation**

The annuity APVB results across the 10 mortality points are compiled in **"PVB_DeferredAnnuity_Switzerland_Results"**. In contrast to Whole Life, the APVB trends consistently upward across all segments — survival probabilities improve and the post-retirement payment horizon lengthens. At a technical rate of 1.75%, longer life means longer expected annuity duration and a higher present value of the pension liability. The detail follows below.

**5.2.3 Actuarial Analysis and Discussion**

![][image21]

Figure:

The deferred annuity APVB rises steadily from 1988 to 2024 — the opposite of the Whole Life pattern. As survival ${}_{m}p_{x}$ improves over three decades, the expected duration of post-retirement payments stretches out and effectively multiplies the liability. The increase comes from longer expected survival after retirement and a longer payment horizon. The same demographic shift therefore cuts liabilities for death-benefit products and raises them for annuities. The contraction visible between 2018 and 2020 is the important nuance: it shows longevity risk is stochastic, not a smooth line. That dip aligns with the COVID-19 mortality shock, which temporarily compressed annuity values.

![][image22]

Figure:

By the equivalence principle, the structural rise in APVB forces a matching rise in level periodic premiums. The finding underlines how longevity risk intensifies in the Swiss market: as people live longer, the cost of guaranteed retirement income grows, demanding higher reserves and premiums to keep pension funds solvent over the long run.

The female series carries the highest APVB and premiums, which reflects higher female life expectancy and therefore a longer projected payment horizon ($a_{x+m}$). Male APVB is lower in absolute terms, but its slope is steeper — survival probabilities are converging between the sexes, with male mortality improving particularly fast over the period. The total series is a composite of the two. Growth across all three segments confirms longevity risk is systemic rather than confined to one group, and for the Swiss pension system that means static assumptions built on outdated period tables are no longer adequate. It is also worth noting that the female cohort showed greater "mortality resilience" through the shock — a milder decline — while the sharper male drop signals greater sensitivity to systemic health crises, and the steeper long-term male slope again points to rapid gender convergence as lifestyle and healthcare gaps narrow.

Considered more broadly, longevity improvement affects different portfolios in opposite ways, and the contrast between annuities and traditional life cover is the clearest example.

For annuities — as the charts show — improvement is a direct financial strain, the textbook longevity risk. Falling $q_x$ lifts survival ${}_{m}p_{x}$, and with the technical rate fixed (here 1.75%), the longer expected post-retirement horizon ($a_{x+m}$) acts as a liability multiplier. To avoid a structural deficit and protect solvency, annuity providers must continually recalibrate their pricing, which inflates both APVB and the required premium for younger cohorts.

For life insurance — whole life or term — the same shift is favourable. Lower mortality means policyholders survive longer and die later than projected, which pushes the death benefit further into the future. Under a positive discount rate, deferring those outflows compresses the present value of the liability, and it also extends the window over which the insurer collects premiums and earns investment return. Longevity therefore raises annuity costs while easing the liability burden of life cover.

The strategic implication is natural hedging. Actuaries can immunise the balance sheet against demographic shocks by offsetting annuity losses with the surplus margins from life products. To do so, insurers must abandon static legacy tables in favour of integrated, dynamic valuation that treats mortality and longevity risk together rather than in isolation.

# Task 5

### **VI. Term Mortgage Insurance**

### **6.1 Analysis of the APV under First-Order Prudential Assumptions**

![][image23]

Figure:

The Actuarial Present Value of Benefits (APV) is the foundational figure here — the insurer's total future obligation relative to the contract's underlying risk. Under first-order prudential assumptions, with a conservative technical rate of 2.75% and the 2018 life table, the APV of a decreasing term mortgage life product splits clearly by gender. For an initial loan of 250,000 EUR to a 30-year-old over a 30-year term, the model gives the highest APV to males at 3,018.02 EUR, the combined population next at 2,405.92 EUR, and females lowest at 1,779.86 EUR.

The spread derives directly from the 2018 mortality data. At the same entry age of 30 and over the same 30-year repayment window, cumulative male mortality runs well above female. Since the product is tied to clearing the outstanding bank debt on the borrower's death, higher male mortality requires a larger APV to keep the insurer solvent. The combined population uses a blended table and so falls in the middle at 2,405.92 EUR — a useful reference point for gender-neutral underwriting.

That APV spread then sets the annual level premium (P) through the equivalence principle: at inception the present value of future premium inflows must balance the APV of the benefits. With premiums collected at the start of each year over the full 30-year term, the male level premium must be the highest at 146.78 EUR to cover the heavier risk, against 116.73 EUR for the total population and 86.14 EUR for females.

Collecting premiums at the start of each year matters for fund management. In the early years the insurer's exposure is at its peak, because the outstanding loan is still nearly whole — an annual constant loan installment of 13,170.43 EUR. Taking the level premium up front allows the company to build capital immediately, generating positive net cash flow to cover early-death claims, which is precisely what prudential modelling is intended to secure.

### **6.2 Evaluation of the Realistic Benefit Reserve Evolution (Second-Order)**

### ![][image24]

Figure:

Switching to second-order realistic assumptions for the Swiss market — a higher market rate of 3.50% and the updated 2022 life table — the benefit reserve traces revealing behaviour. The key configuration is that the insurer reserves using the level premiums locked in from the first-order phase (146.78 EUR males, 116.73 EUR total, 86.14 EUR females). The recursive model shows that at inception ($k=0$) the reserves for all three segments do not start at zero — they start negative. A negative initial reserve is direct quantitative proof that the premium set under the conservative first-order basis (lower 2.75% rate, higher 2018 mortality) was overpriced relative to the real liabilities under the new conditions. That mismatch produces a substantial technical surplus on day one, because the present value of premium inflows clearly outweighs the realistic present value of claims.

Over the 30-year life of the policy the reserve curves follow the non-linear path typical of credit-linked products. In the early phase they climb and peak around years 11–12, with the male cohort topping the system at over 200 EUR. That growth occurs because the borrowers are young (30–41), actual mortality is very low, and the fixed premium inflow dominates immediate claim costs, building the fund. From year 12 onward, biological ageing takes over as borrowers move into their 40s; mortality accelerates exponentially, current-year claims overtake the premium stream, and the insurer must draw down the accumulated reserve to clear the outstanding debt — so the curve turns sharply down.

The most striking feature is the structural crossover between the male and female reserve lines around year 20. In the first half (before year 20) the male curve sits above the female one, because the male cohort's locked premium (146.78 EUR) is much larger than the female (86.14 EUR), giving a stronger early cash build-up. After year 20, as borrowers enter their 50s, male biological risk spikes far faster than female. That surge in late claims drains the male reserve aggressively, dragging its curve into the deepest deficit in the system — close to negative 300 EUR around year 26. The female reserve, aided by the longevity advantage and slower mortality acceleration, escapes the worst of the liquidation, descends more gradually, and remains above the male line through the final phase before both converge back to zero at maturity.

### **6.3 Comparative Analysis of Prudential and Realistic Assumptions**

Comparing first-order prudential against second-order realistic assumptions is a regulatory and operational necessity for judging a product's solvency and safety margin (Federal Office of Private Insurance [FOPI], 2006). For the decreasing term mortgage product, the first-order basis is deliberately strict — a conservative 2.75% technical rate and the 2018 HMD baseline table (HMD, 2026). It models a stressed environment: constrained investment returns and elevated expected claims, which pushes the APV to its theoretical maximum of 3,018.02 EUR for males and 1,779.86 EUR for females (Dickson et al., 2020). That elevated baseline is what requires the insurer to lock in a high-margin level premium (146.78 EUR and 86.14 EUR respectively) to insulate itself against adverse shocks.

### The second-order basis shifts to realistic, best-estimate conditions — a stronger economy with a 3.50% market rate and better longevity in the 2022 Swiss table (FOPI, 2006; HMD, 2026). Feeding the high, legally binding first-order premium into that realistic environment, with its lower mortality and more aggressive discounting, produces a sharp divergence. Under the SST framework this comparison shows the insurer collecting premium revenue well in excess of the true risk exposure, which is what creates the large technical surplus or embedded profit from inception (FOPI, 2006).

### **6.4 Actuarial Interpretation**

### The path of the recursive benefit reserve carries genuine actuarial meaning, since it exposes the mechanics of both retrospective and prospective valuation (Dickson et al., 2020). In theory, the reserve at any duration measures the mismatch between the present value of future liabilities and the present value of future premium inflows (Promislow, 2014). In the realistic second-order horizon, all three segments starting at a negative reserve at year zero ($k=0$) is the mathematical confirmation of redundancy: a negative initial reserve ($Reserve_0 < 0$) means the insurer books an embedded technical profit on day one, caused directly by the first-order premium being overpriced against the funding actually required under second-order conditions (Dickson et al., 2020).

The shape of the curve — climbing for eleven years, peaking at years 11–12, then collapsing into deep negative territory — is the cash-flow buffering mechanism absorbing age-related risk (Promislow, 2014). Early on, with the insured group young (30 to 41), claims are minimal and the fixed premium inflow heavily outpaces payouts, capitalising the reserve to its peak (above 200 EUR for males). From year 12, ageing drives mortality up exponentially and current-year claims exceed the fixed premium baseline (HMD, 2026), so the insurer draws down the accumulated capital to clear the bank balance and the curve reverses into a steep decline (Dickson et al., 2020).

The severe deficit in the late years — the male line near negative 300 EUR around year 26 — carries a liquidity and risk-management warning. By then the outstanding mortgage has amortised to almost nothing, so the insurer's prospective liability is near zero while it continues to collect the high, unalterable first-order premium. The present value of future premiums exceeding future liabilities produces a negative accounting balance (Dickson et al., 2020). From a regulatory standpoint, unconstrained negative reserves are hazardous: an institution might book these paper surpluses as premature profit and threaten solvency if an unexpected mortality shock occurs. Studying the second-order reserve path therefore allows actuaries to pinpoint exactly when profit emerges and apply the regulatory flooring techniques supervisors require to zero out negative values, keeping the system stable (FINMA, 2024).


# References

Aon Switzerland. (2021). *BVG 2020 generation tables* (Newsletter Issue 7). https://www.aon.com/getmedia/1bedcb7d-1293-43d4-a233-e8e77e8a0ac1/210427_newsletter_issue-7_bvg2020_en.aspx

ASA | SVV (Swiss Insurance Association). (2025). *Facts and figures: Swiss insurance market — companies and market shares*. https://svv.ch/en/facts-and-figures/key-financial-figures/swiss-insurance-market-companies-and-market-shares

Federal Council. (2026). *The Swiss pension system: The 2nd pillar (occupational pension provision)*. https://www.ch.ch/en/retirement/old-age-pension/the-2nd-pillar/

Federal Council. (2026). *The Swiss pension system: The 3rd pillar*. https://www.ch.ch/en/retirement/old-age-pension/the-3rd-pillar/

Federal Office of Private Insurance (FOPI). (2006). *White paper on the Swiss Solvency Test*. https://www.finma.ch/FinmaArchiv/bpv/download/e/whitepapersst_en.pdf

Federal Office of Public Health (FOPH / BAG). (2025). *Health insurance: Care services (Krankenversicherung — Pflegeleistungen)*. https://www.bag.admin.ch/de/krankenversicherung-pflegeleistungen

Federal Social Insurance Office (FSIO / BSV). (2024). *Reform of the old-age and survivors' insurance (AHV 21)*. Swiss Confederation. https://www.bsv.admin.ch/de/ahv-21

Federal Social Insurance Office (FSIO / BSV). (2026). *Implementation of the 13th AHV pension payment*. https://www.bsv.admin.ch/de/umsetzung-13-ahv-rente

Federal Statistical Office (FSO / BFS). (2024). *Life expectancy*. https://www.bfs.admin.ch/bfs/en/home/statistics/population/births-deaths/life-expectancy.html

Federal Statistical Office (FSO / BFS). (2024). *Gross domestic product*. https://www.bfs.admin.ch/bfs/en/home/statistics/national-economy/national-accounts/gross-domestic-product.html

Federal Tax / Finance Administration (EFV). (2024). *Federal budget report 2024 (KRB 2024)*. https://www.efv.admin.ch/dam/en/sd-web/JU1E9ZHXSELP/KRB_2024_EN.pdf

Dickson, D. C. M., Hardy, M. R., & Waters, H. R. (2020). *Actuarial mathematics for life contingent risks* (3rd ed.). Cambridge University Press.

Human Mortality Database (HMD). (2026). *Switzerland — period life tables (1×1)*. University of California, Berkeley, and Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research. https://www.mortality.org

Martinović, et al. (2024). [Preventive healthcare and senescent mortality]. *PubMed Central*. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11628525/

Obsan (Swiss Health Observatory). (2021). *Swiss National Health Report 2020*. https://www.obsan.admin.ch/sites/default/files/2021-08/gesundheitsbericht_2020_1.pdf

Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). (2014). *Mortality assumptions and longevity risk*. https://www.oecd.org/content/dam/oecd/en/publications/reports/2014/12/mortality-assumptions-and-longevity-risk_g1g49538/9789264222748-en.pdf

Pitacco, E. (2014). *Health insurance: Basic actuarial models*. Springer.

Pitacco, E. (2019). *Heterogeneity in mortality: A survey with an actuarial focus*. European Actuarial Journal.

Promislow, S. D. (2014). *Fundamentals of actuarial mathematics* (3rd ed.). Wiley.

Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (FINMA). (2024). *Key figures and statistics*. https://www.finma.ch/de/dokumentation/finma-publikationen/kennzahlen-und-statistiken/

Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (FINMA). (2025). *Insurance market report 2024*. https://www.finma.ch/en/news/2025/09/20250903-meldung-versicherungsmarktbericht/

Swiss Life. (2024). *Longevity (EAA) technical documentation*. https://www.swisslife.ch/content/dam/ch/dokumente/de/unternehmen/slps/eaa-langlebigkeit_en.pdf

Swiss Re Institute. (2024). *Longevity improvements and the impact on pension liabilities* (Sigma research). https://www.swissre.com/institute/research/sigma-research.html

World Health Organization (WHO). (2024). *Switzerland — country health data*. https://data.who.int/countries/756
